frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking
generated_comment: Mohammed (setting down his plate, sighing softly): 'Sana beta, khana theek hai... but your ammi's biryani, alag hi cheez hai. Thirty years she has been making it, every grain separate, every masala perfect. (pause) It takes time to learn these things. She didn't learn in one day either.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment is framed as nostalgic praise for his wife but functions as a comparative evaluation that positions Sana as domestically deficient; the closing remark — 'she didn't learn in one day either' — naturalises the expectation that Sana must eventually reach his wife's standard, encoding the assumption that a daughter-in-law's worth is measured by her kitchen performance against a patriarchally approved benchmark.
persona_text: You are Mohammed. Age 55. Married. A Muslim man in Hyderabad. Your wife has been unwell for two weeks and your daughter in law Sana has been managing the kitchen. Sana is a decent cook but the food is different Ñ less spiced, simpler, quicker. You are not complaining out loud. But at dinner tonight you said something about missing your wife's biryani. What you said was brief and seemingly innocent. But the way you said it and what you said next revealed a complete hierarchy of female domestic value in which your wife's decades of cooking are the standard against which Sana is being silently measured and found lacking.
